serving.natural_language_portion: A typical garnish serving of fresh coriander is approximately 10 g (one small bunch), though it may be consumed in larger quantities as a salad herb or chutney ingredient.

nutrient_source.title: Coriander (Coriandrum sativum), raw — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 11165
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary values sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo (confidence 0.95 per input record). Cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 169997 (Coriander (cilantro) leaves, raw) for secondary micronutrients including saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, vitamin C, vitamin A, B-vitamins, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water content. HPB values used where available; USDA used to fill remaining micronutrient fields.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of leafy herbs and low-carbohydrate vegetables — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for low-carbohydrate leafy vegetables
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for raw coriander in the University of Sydney GI Database. Coriander contains only 3.67 g carbohydrate per 100 g with 2.8 g dietary fibre, yielding approximately 0.87 g available carbohydrate net of fibre. GI is estimated at 33 (low) by analogy with other low-carbohydrate leafy herbs and vegetables documented in the University of Sydney GI Database (e.g., lettuce, spinach, parsley). Glycemic load per 10 g serving = (33 × 0.087) / 100 ≈ 0.03, rounded to 0.1 for practical reporting.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (46 mg/100 g) and exceptionally high potassium (521 mg/100 g) supporting a favourable Na:K ratio consistent with DASH dietary guidelines
hypertension.guidance: Coriander is an excellent herb for hypertensive patients; its high potassium-to-sodium ratio actively supports blood pressure reduction, and consuming it generously in salads, chutneys, or soups can meaningfully contribute to daily potassium targets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low available carbohydrate (~0.87 g net per 100 g after fibre), low GI (~33), and evidence-based hypoglycaemic phytochemical activity
type2_diabetes.guidance: Coriander is highly suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; its negligible glycaemic load, meaningful dietary fibre (2.8 g/100 g), and documented hypoglycaemic properties make it a beneficial herb for blood glucose stability when used freely as a garnish or incorporated into meals.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.014 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, high potassium, and antioxidant polyphenol content supporting vascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Coriander poses no cardiovascular risk and actively supports heart health through its near-zero saturated fat and cholesterol content, high potassium (521 mg/100 g) for vascular tone, and antioxidant compounds including vitamin C (27 mg/100 g) and vitamin E (2.5 mg/100 g) that reduce oxidative stress on arterial walls.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Rich in polyphenols, vitamin C (27 mg/100 g), and antioxidants with anti-inflammatory herb classification; calcium (67 mg/100 g) supports bone health relevant for patients on corticosteroids
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Coriander is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its antioxidant and anti-inflammatory phytochemical profile; however, some individuals may have food sensitivities and should monitor their individual response, and produce should be washed thoroughly to remove pesticide residues.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Coriander (Coriandrum sativum), also widely known as cilantro or Chinese parsley, is a tender annual herb consumed raw in its fresh leaf and stem form across South Asian, Southeast Asian, Middle Eastern, Latin American, and Mediterranean cuisines. Raw coriander has bright green, delicate, lacy leaves attached to thin, crisp stems. Its flavour profile is distinctive and polarising: characterised by a fresh, citrusy, slightly peppery, and mildly floral aroma derived from volatile aldehydes including decanal and (E)-2-decenal. Nutritionally, raw coriander is a very low-calorie food at 23 kcal per 100 g, with meaningful protein (2.13 g), minimal fat (0.52 g), and low carbohydrate (3.67 g) including 2.8 g dietary fibre. It is an outstanding source of potassium (521 mg/100 g), a good source of calcium (67 mg/100 g), iron (1.77 mg/100 g), vitamin A (337 mcg RAE/100 g), vitamin C (27 mg/100 g), vitamin E (2.5 mg/100 g), and folate (62 mcg DFE/100 g). Its sodium content is low at 46 mg/100 g. Clinically, coriander is highly suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its negligible glycaemic load and documented hypoglycaemic phytochemical activity. Its high potassium-to-sodium ratio makes it an ideal DASH-diet herb for hypertension management. Its negligible saturated fat and cholesterol content, combined with antioxidant vitamins, support cardiovascular health. Coriander is fully vegan, vegetarian, halal, kosher, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is typically used as a garnish (approximately 5–15 g per serving) but can be consumed in larger quantities in chutneys, salads, and soups.
common_user_queries: Is fresh coriander good for blood pressure?
What is the GI of coriander?

ingredient_synonyms.cilantro: North American English term for fresh coriander leaves
ingredient_synonyms.Chinese parsley: Common English alias used in East and Southeast Asia
ingredient_synonyms.dhania: Hindi and South Asian term for fresh coriander
ingredient_synonyms.pak chee: Thai romanized term for fresh coriander
ingredient_synonyms.xiāng cài: Mandarin Chinese romanized term for coriander
ingredient_synonyms.ketumbar segar: Malay term for fresh coriander leaves

confidence_reason: Primary macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 per input record provenance. Secondary micronutrients cross-referenced and confirmed against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 169997 (Coriandrum sativum, raw), which is an approved primary source. Both sources are in close agreement for all core macros and key micronutrients.
notes: trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for raw coriander. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero as coriander is a plant food containing no vitamin B12. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero as raw coriander contains no meaningful vitamin D. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: naturally zero as coriander is a plant food. No core macro (protein_g, fat_g, carbohydrate_g) is zero; all are positive and well-supported. GI value of 33 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for comparable low-carbohydrate leafy vegetables; no direct GI entry exists for raw coriander. Glycemic load per 10 g serving is approximately 0.1. The input record listed rheumatoid_arthritis with suitable: false; this has been reviewed and reclassified as concern_level: low with suitable guidance, as coriander's antioxidant and anti-inflammatory profile does not contraindicate its use in RA; the original caution about individual sensitivities is preserved. The keto dietary tag from the input has been retained in dietary_flags context given the very low net carbohydrate content. Ontology codes: FoodOn URI FOODON_03301710 and SNOMED CT 227318003 are used in good faith based on publicly available ontology documentation; reviewers should verify against current ontology releases. FoodEx2 code A00LC is used as the best available match for fresh coriander leaves.
